prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 27-year-old man.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: Over the last month, I've noticed bright red blood on the toilet paper a few times after bowel movements. It's not mixed into the stool. I've also been constipated and straining sometimes. No abdominal pain, no black stools, and no weight loss.
 - History: No family history of colon cancer that I know of.
 - Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 66
 - Exam: No exam done yet.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Hemoglobin: 14.4 g/dL (13.5-17.5) normal
 - MCV: 90 fL (80-100) normal
 - Ferritin: 60 ng/mL (30-400) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your lab results are all completely normal. Your hemoglobin is healthy, meaning you're not anemic from blood loss. Your MCV is normal, ruling out the type of red blood cell changes seen with chronic bleeding. Your ferritin (iron stores) is also normal, which further confirms you're not losing significant amounts of blood. The bright red blood on the toilet paper — not mixed into the stool — combined with straining and constipation is a very common pattern in young adults and most often points to hemorrhoids or a small anal fissure (a tiny tear from straining). These are not dangerous conditions. That said, any rectal bleeding deserves a proper evaluation by your doctor, including a physical exam, to confirm the cause and rule out anything else. There's no sign of emergency here — no anemia, no black stools, no weight loss, and stable vitals.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 95%

Your MCV is normal, ruling out the type of red blood cell changes seen with chronic bleeding. Your ferritin (iron stores) is also normal, which further confirms you're not losing significant amounts of blood over time. The bright red blood on the toilet paper — not mixed into the stool — combined with straining and constipation is a very common pattern in young adults and most often points to hemorrhoids or a small anal fissure (a tiny tear from straining). These are not dangerous conditions. That said, any rectal bleeding deserves a proper evaluation by your doctor, including a visual or digital rectal exam, to confirm the cause and rule out anything less common. There's no sign of an emergency here, but don't ignore it indefinitely.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 95%
